title: Walks
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $v,w \in G$.

  1. A walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a finite alternating sequence of adjacent nodes and edges \[v=v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n=w\]

    in $G$ for some $n \ge 0$, i.e.\ such that for every $k=0,\dots,n-1$ we have that $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \cup L$ or $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$, and with end nodes $v_0=v$ and $v_n=w$. An example walk from $v_0$ to $v_3$ could look like: \[v_0 \tuh v_1 \hut v_2 \huh v_3, \qquad\text{ with } \qquad v_0 \tuh v_1, v_2 \tuh v_1 \in E,\,v_2 \huh v_3 \in L. \] The same node may appear multiple times in a walk. Also the trivial walk consisting of a single node $v_0 \in G$ is allowed (if $v=w$). The walk is called into $v_0$ if $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$, and out of $v_0$ if $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$. Similarly, it is called into $v_n$ if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$ and out of $v_n$ if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$.

  2. A directed walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form: \[v=v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\] for some $n \ge 0$, where all arrowheads point in the direction of $w$ and there are no arrowheads pointing back.
  3. A bidirected walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form: \[v=v_0 \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh v_n=w,\] for some $n \ge 0$, where all edges are bidirected.
  4. A collider walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form: \[v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w,\] for some $n \ge 0$, where all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them (a.k.a.\ collider). Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$.
  5. A walk is called path if no node occurs more than once.
  6. A bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk of the form: \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]

    such that $v \ne w$, the walk contains both endnodes exactly once, the subwalk $v_0 \hut \cdots v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$, and the subwalk $v_k \tuh \cdots v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$.

    If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has source $v_{k}$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the edge classifications of def def-edge-relations. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$.

  1. Walk. A walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is, for some integer $n \ge 0$, a tuple \[ (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] consisting of $n + 1$ vertices $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$ and $n$ ordered pairs $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1} \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$, such that:
    1. $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$;
    2. for every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr). \]

    The case $n = 0$ is admitted: the trivial walk is the single-vertex tuple $(v_0)$ with $v_0 = v = w$, no edges, and condition (b) vacuously satisfied; it is a walk only when $v = w$. For $n \ge 1$, vertices in the tuple may repeat, i.e.\ the same node may appear multiple times among $v_0, \dots, v_n$.

    End-node classifications. For a non-trivial walk ($n \ge 1$) we further classify behaviour at the two end-nodes:

    • the walk is into $v_0$ iff $a_0$ is an edge into $v_0$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; concretely, iff $\bigl(a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L\bigr)$;
    • the walk is out of $v_0$ iff $a_0$ is an edge out of $v_0$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item iii.; concretely, iff $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in E$;
    • the walk is into $v_n$ iff $a_{n-1}$ is an edge into $v_n$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; concretely, iff $\bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L\bigr)$;
    • the walk is out of $v_n$ iff $a_{n-1}$ is an edge out of $v_n$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item iii.; concretely, iff $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$.

    On the trivial walk ($n = 0$) all four classifications are vacuously false (neither $a_0$ nor $a_{n-1}$ exists). On a non-trivial walk with a bidirected first edge ($a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$) the walk is into $v_0$ but not out of $v_0$; hence “into $v_0$” and “out of $v_0$” are mutually exclusive but not jointly exhaustive over walks (and analogously at $v_n$).

  2. Directed walk. A directed walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) that, in addition, satisfies \[ a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \qquad \text{for every } k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}. \] The trivial walk ($n = 0$, $v = w$) is admitted as a directed walk from $v$ to itself.
  3. Bidirected walk. A bidirected walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) that, in addition, satisfies \[ a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L \qquad \text{for every } k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\} \] (equivalently, by the symmetry of $L$ from def def-cdmg, $(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in L$ for every such $k$). The trivial walk ($n = 0$, $v = w$) is admitted as a bidirected walk from $v$ to itself.
  4. Collider walk. A collider walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) satisfying the case-distinguished constraints below.

    • Case $n = 0$: no further constraint; the trivial walk is a collider walk from $v$ to itself when $v = w$.
    • Case $n = 1$: the single edge $a_0$ is required to be bidirected, namely \[ a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L \qquad \text{(equivalently, } (v, w) \in L\text{).} \] Purely directed edges $(v, w) \in E$ or $(w, v) \in E$ are not admitted as collider walks of length $1$.
    • Case $n \ge 2$: the edges satisfy: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
    • (the first edge places an arrowhead at $v_1$) $\bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L\bigr)$;
    • (every interior edge is bidirected) for every $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, n - 2\}$, $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$;
    • (the last edge places an arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$) $\bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L\bigr)$. \end{enumerate} Under (a)–(c), every interior node $v_k$ (i.e.\ $1 \le k \le n - 1$) has an arrowhead pointing toward it from each of its two incident walk-edges $a_{k-1}$ and $a_k$, recovering the verbal characterisation “every node strictly between $v$ and $w$ is a collider”.

    Reconciliation with the source block's $n = 1$ inline note. The source block contains the inline remark “Note that for $n = 1$ this reads: $v \sus w \in G$”. This remark is overridden by the strict reading of Case $n = 1$ above: for $n = 1$, a collider walk requires its unique edge to be bidirected, $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$. The relaxed reading “any $v \sus w$ edge” is not adopted here; in particular, neither $(v, w) \in E$ nor $(w, v) \in E$ qualifies as a collider walk of length $1$.

  5. Path. A walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is called a path iff its vertex sequence contains no repetitions, i.e.\ $v_i \ne v_j$ whenever $0 \le i < j \le n$. (Equivalently, the tuple $(v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n)$ consists of $n + 1$ pairwise distinct entries. The trivial walk $n = 0$ is vacuously a path.)
  6. Bifurcation. A bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ for which there exists an index $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$ (in particular $n \ge 1$) such that all of the following hold:

    1. Distinct end-nodes appearing exactly once. $v \ne w$, and the end-nodes each occur exactly once in the vertex sequence: \[ v_0 \notin \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\} \qquad\text{and}\qquad v_n \notin \{v_0, v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}\}. \]
    2. Left subwalk is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$. For every $j \in \{0, 1, \dots, k - 2\}$, \[ a_j = (v_{j+1}, v_j) \in E. \] (When $k = 1$ the left subwalk is the trivial walk $(v_0)$ and this constraint is vacuous.)
    3. Right subwalk is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$. For every $j \in \{k, k + 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, \[ a_j = (v_j, v_{j+1}) \in E. \] (When $k = n$ the right subwalk is the trivial walk $(v_n)$ and this constraint is vacuous.)
    4. Middle (hinge) edge. The edge $a_{k-1}$ between $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ satisfies \[ \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L\bigr). \]
    5. (Addition: each end-node has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it.) $a_0$ is an edge into $v_0$ and $a_{n-1}$ is an edge into $v_n$, in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; equivalently, both end-nodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ have exactly one arrowhead pointing toward them (contributed by their unique incident walk-edges $a_0$ and $a_{n-1}$ respectively, uniqueness following from clause (a)).

      Spelled out as a case analysis on $k$:

      • if $k \ge 2$: clause (b) gives $a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E$, which is automatically an edge into $v_0$ (no further restriction);
      • if $k = 1$: $a_0 = a_{k-1}$ is the middle edge; both alternatives admitted by (d) — namely $a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E$ and $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$ — are edges into $v_0$, so no further restriction at $v_0$;
      • if $k \le n - 1$: clause (c) gives $a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E$, which is automatically an edge into $v_n$ (no further restriction);
      • if $k = n$: $a_{n-1} = a_{k-1}$ is the middle edge; among the two alternatives admitted by (d), only the bidirected alternative $a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L$ is an edge into $v_n$, whereas the directed alternative $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$ places its arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$ rather than $v_n$ and is therefore excluded.

      In particular, the degenerate configuration “$k = n$ together with the directed hinge $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$” — which would otherwise admit a tuple of the form $v_n \to v_{n-1} \to \cdots \to v_1 \to v_0$ (a plain directed walk from $v_n$ to $v_0$) as a “bifurcation” — is not a bifurcation under the present definition.

    Source of the bifurcation. If clause (d) is realised by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, then we call $v_k$ the source of the bifurcation. If clause (d) is realised by the bidirected alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$, no source is defined. Combined with clause (e), whenever a source $v_k$ is defined we automatically have $1 \le k \le n - 1$ (the case $k = n$ with directed hinge is excluded by (e)), so the source $v_k$ is an interior vertex of the walk and is distinct from both end-nodes $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$.

lean_blocks.explanation: Section-wide `variable` declaration. Lean 4's `variable` mechanism makes `Node` and its `DecidableEq` instance available as auto-bound implicit arguments to every declaration below: any later `def`/`inductive` that mentions `Node` will silently pick up `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` in its signature. `Type*` is the universe-polymorphic flavour (Lean 4 inserts a fresh universe variable), letting `Node` live in any universe. The `DecidableEq Node` typeclass is needed downstream to compute `Finset`-membership of edges in `CDMG`, to use `List.Nodup` in `IsPath`, and to call `List.tail` / `dropLast` / `getLast?` on vertex lists. This sets the type-level stage for the rest of the row.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide implicit binders, auto-added to every later declaration
-- `Type*` is universe-polymorphic; `DecidableEq` enables Finset/List ops
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def WalkStep (G : CDMG Node) (u : Node) (a : Node × Node) (v : Node) : Prop :=
  (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∨ (a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E)
lean_blocks.explanation: Helper predicate factoring the LN's per-edge constraint. `G.WalkStep u a v` says the ordered pair `a` is a valid walk-step from `u` to `v`: either `a` is the *forward* writing `(u, v)` with `a ∈ E ∪ L` (an edge directed or bidirected from `u` to `v`), or `a` is the *backward* writing `(v, u)` with `a ∈ E` (a directed edge from `v` back to `u`, traversed against its direction). Note `G.E`, `G.L` are `Finset (Node × Node)` fields of the CDMG. A fourth backward-`L` case is omitted because `hL_symm` (symmetry of `L`) collapses it into the forward `L`-disjunct. This predicate is fed as a hypothesis to `Walk.cons` below, so every edge in a walk carries a structural witness of validity.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Per-edge validity predicate, used as the `h` hypothesis in `Walk.cons`
def WalkStep (G : CDMG Node) (u : Node) (a : Node × Node) (v : Node) : Prop :=
  -- Forward writing: directed or bidirected edge from `u` to `v`
  (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∨ (a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E)

lean_blocks.explanation: Namespace-level `variable` declaration binding `G` implicitly. Every declaration inside the `Walk` namespace below (vertex/edge extractors, four end-node classifiers, walk-class predicates, bifurcation helpers) takes a walk of type `Walk G u v`, and Lean's auto-bound `variable` mechanism inserts `{G : CDMG Node}` into each of their signatures. Making `G` implicit lets downstream callers use dot-notation (`p.vertices` rather than `Walk.vertices G p`) since `G` is recoverable from the walk's type. The three-dash `-- def_3_4 --- start helper` marker used in the source file (visible in the surrounding comments) distinguishes this load-bearing infrastructure binder from the two-dash markers used on actual statement bodies.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Namespace-wide implicit `G`, inferred from `Walk G u v` at call sites
variable {G : CDMG Node}

lean_blocks.code:
def vertices : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node
  | _, _, .nil v _ => [v]
  | u, _, .cons _ _ _ p => u :: p.vertices
lean_blocks.explanation: Recursive function extracting the vertex sequence `[v_0, v_1, …, v_n]` from a walk. The signature `∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node` with implicit endpoints lets callers write `p.vertices` directly. Two pattern-match cases on the `Walk` inductive: a trivial walk `.nil v _` (the single-vertex walk) returns `[v]`; a `cons` step `.cons _ _ _ p` with current start vertex `u` (matched as the first binder of the implicit `∀`) prepends `u` to the recursive vertex list of the tail `p`. This produces a `List Node` of length `n + 1`, matching the LN's tuple. Using `List` (not `Finset` or `Fin n → Node`) preserves order and duplicates and unlocks `List.Nodup` for `IsPath`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Extracts the LN's vertex tuple `(v_0, v_1, …, v_n)` as a `List Node`
def vertices : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node
  -- Trivial walk: a single-vertex list containing just the endpoint
  | _, _, .nil v _ => [v]
  -- Cons step: prepend current start vertex `u`, recurse on the tail `p`
  | u, _, .cons _ _ _ p => u :: p.vertices

lean_blocks.explanation: Recursive function extracting the edge sequence `[a_0, a_1, …, a_{n-1}]` from a walk, as a `List (Node × Node)`. Mirrors `vertices` but is one element shorter: the trivial walk `.nil _ _` has no edges and returns `[]`; a `cons` cell `.cons _ a _ p` (where `a : Node × Node` is the step's edge pair) prepends `a` to the recursive edge list of `p`. The empty list on `nil` is what makes `List.getLast?` return `none` on the trivial walk, which in turn powers `intoEnd` / `outOfEnd` returning `False` vacuously. Splitting vertices and edges into two parallel lists (rather than one alternating list) matches the LN presentation and lets each downstream predicate touch only the data it needs.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Extracts the LN's edge tuple `(a_0, a_1, …, a_{n-1})` as a list of pairs
def edges : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List (Node × Node)
  -- Trivial walk has no edges
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => []
  -- Cons step: prepend the current edge `a`, recurse on tail `p`
  | _, _, .cons _ a _ p => a :: p.edges

lean_blocks.code:
def IsColliderRest : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) =>
      (a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E) ∨ (a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L)
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsColliderRest
lean_blocks.explanation: Helper predicate carrying the *interior + last-edge* half of an `n ≥ 2` collider walk after its first edge has been consumed. Three branches: (i) on `.nil _ _` (unreachable from the only call site, set to `True` for totality); (ii) on a single remaining edge `cons v a _ (.nil _ _)` this *is* the LN's last edge `a_{n-1}`, which must place an arrowhead at the current start `u` (i.e. at `v_{n-1}` in LN naming) — encoded as the disjunction `(v, u) ∈ E ∨ (u, v) ∈ L`; (iii) on a longer tail `cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _))` this is a true *interior* edge `a_k`, required to be forward-bidirected (`a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L`), and the rest must recursively satisfy `IsColliderRest`. Note the `p@(.cons _ _ _ _)` pattern is Lean's *as-pattern* binding the inner `cons` to `p` while matching its shape.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Tail predicate for collider walks: interior edges bidirected, last edge into `v_{n-1}`
def IsColliderRest : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Unreachable from caller; vacuously `True` for totality
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- Last edge of the walk: arrowhead at current start vertex `u` (LN's `v_{n-1}`)
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) =>
      (a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E) ∨ (a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L)
  -- Interior edge: must be forward-bidirected; recurse on tail using as-pattern `p@…`
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsColliderRest

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcationWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _), 0 => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0 =>
      ((a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E) ∨ (a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p, k + 1 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsBifurcationWithSplit k
lean_blocks.explanation: Helper predicate parametrised by a split-index counter `i : ℕ`, where `i + 1` is the LN's bifurcation hinge position `k`. Recurses jointly on the walk and the counter: (i) `nil`, any `i`: `False` (a bifurcation needs at least the hinge edge); (ii) `cons v a _ (.nil _ _), 0` — the *first edge* is also the *last* (`n = 1`, `k = n`), so by the operator addition the hinge must be bidirected: `a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L`; (iii) `cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0` — the first edge *is* the hinge but the right arm is non-trivial, so both hinge alternatives (`(v,u) ∈ E` directed or `(u,v) ∈ L` bidirected) are admitted, and the rest must be a directed walk; (iv) `cons v a _ p, k + 1` — the first edge is a *left-arm* edge, reverse-directed `(v, u) ∈ E`, and we recurse on the tail with counter `k`. The `ℕ`-counter avoids list-indexing bounds plumbing.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Bifurcation predicate parametrised by `i = k - 1` (split position in edges)
def IsBifurcationWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  -- Empty walk cannot be a bifurcation at any split
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  -- n=1, k=n case: hinge is also the only edge; must be bidirected per addition
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _), 0 => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  -- n≥2, k=1 case: hinge admits both directed-backward and bidirected; right arm is directed
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0 =>
      ((a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E) ∨ (a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
  -- Left-arm edge: reverse-directed `(v, u) ∈ E`; decrement counter and recurse
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p, k + 1 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsBifurcationWithSplit k

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p, k + 1 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit k
lean_blocks.explanation: Variant of `IsBifurcationWithSplit` restricting the hinge to its *directed* alternative (LN clause (d) first disjunct, `(v_k, v_{k-1}) ∈ E`), which is the LN's precondition for a bifurcation to have a *source*. Differs from the general helper in two branches: (i) the `cons _ _ _ (.nil _ _), 0` case is now `False` rather than admitting a bidirected hinge — by the operator addition, an `n = 1` `k = n` directed-hinge walk has its arrowhead at `v_{n-1}` not `v_n`, so `v_n` lacks the required arrowhead; (ii) the `n ≥ 2` hinge case pins the directed form `(v, u) ∈ E` alone, dropping the bidirected `(u, v) ∈ L` disjunct. The left-arm recursive case is identical to the general helper. Used by `IsBifurcationSource` to identify the unique vertex `v_{i+1}` deserving the LN's source label.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Directed-hinge variant of `IsBifurcationWithSplit`; powers `IsBifurcationSource`
def IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  -- Empty walk: no bifurcation, no source
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  -- n=1, k=n with directed hinge would place arrowhead at v_0, excluded by addition
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  -- n≥2 directed hinge `(v_k, v_{k-1}) ∈ E`; right arm must be directed
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
  -- Left-arm step: same reverse-directed edge as in the general helper
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p, k + 1 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit k

lean_blocks.code:
inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  | nil (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node) (a : Node × Node)
      (h : G.WalkStep u a v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w
lean_blocks.explanation: The central `inductive Type` of the row. `Walk G u v` is the type of walks from `u` to `v` in `G`, with both endpoints baked into the index of the type (so the trivial walk lives at `Walk G v v`, enforcing the LN's `v = w` precondition at the type level). The constructors mirror the LN's two presentations: `nil v hv` is the *trivial walk* `(v_0)` and carries an explicit membership witness `hv : v ∈ G` since for `n = 0` no edge is around to recover `v ∈ J ∪ V` from. `cons v a h p` prepends one alternating step `v_0, a_0, v_1` in front of an existing walk `p : Walk G v w`; the middle vertex `v` is made *explicit* (departing from `SimpleGraph.Walk`) so downstream pattern matches reach it without `(v := …)` named-pattern syntax. The hypothesis `h : G.WalkStep u a v` is the per-edge LN constraint.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- The walk type, indexed by source/target nodes; mirrors mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk`
inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  -- Trivial walk `(v_0)`; needs explicit `hv : v ∈ G` since no edge witnesses it
  | nil (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  -- Cons step: prepend `(v_0, a_0, v_1)` to an existing walk; middle `v` is explicit
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node) (a : Node × Node)
      (h : G.WalkStep u a v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w

lean_blocks.code:
def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.into u a
lean_blocks.explanation: End-node classifier predicate: `p.intoStart` is true iff `p` is non-trivial AND its first edge `a_0` is an edge *into* `v_0` in the sense of def_3_3 item ii (i.e. an arrowhead points at `v_0`). Recursion is direct: the trivial walk `.nil _ _` returns `False` (no `a_0` exists, so the LN clause is vacuously not satisfied), and the `cons _ a _ _` case delegates to `G.into u a` from `EdgeRelations.lean` — keeping the LN-macro-grep correspondence. The four classifiers (`intoStart`, `outOfStart`, `intoEnd`, `outOfEnd`) are deliberately encoded as four *independent* `Prop`s rather than a four-way enum, because they are not jointly exhaustive: a bidirected first edge yields `intoStart ∧ ¬outOfStart`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Walk classifier: is the first edge `a_0` an edge into `v_0`?
def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk has no first edge; LN's `a_0` does not exist
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  -- Delegate to `G.into u a` from EdgeRelations.lean (def_3_3 item ii)
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.into u a

lean_blocks.code:
def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.outOf u a
lean_blocks.explanation: Mirror of `intoStart` for the opposite end-node behaviour at `v_0`: `p.outOfStart` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its first edge `a_0` is an edge *out of* `v_0` (def_3_3 item iii), i.e. `a_0 = (v_0, v_1) ∈ E` — a directed edge pointing away from `v_0`. Because `G.outOf` excludes `L`-edges entirely (per def_3_3), a bidirected first edge satisfies `intoStart` but not `outOfStart` — the two classifiers are mutually exclusive on `E`-edges but jointly *not* exhaustive over all walks. The trivial walk returns `False` by the same vacuity argument as `intoStart`. The recursion shape (direct `nil`/`cons` match, delegate to `G.outOf` on the first edge) is identical.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Walk classifier: is the first edge `a_0` an edge out of `v_0`?
def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously not out of anything
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  -- Delegate to `G.outOf u a`; note `outOf` excludes L-edges per def_3_3 iii
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.outOf u a

lean_blocks.code:
def intoEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.into v a
lean_blocks.explanation: End-node classifier for the *right* end of the walk: `p.intoEnd` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its last edge `a_{n-1}` is an edge into `v_n` (def_3_3 item ii). Unlike `intoStart`, this is *not* recursive on the walk — there is no direct constructor pattern for the last `cons` cell — so we use `p.edges.getLast?` to peek at the final edge as an `Option (Node × Node)`. A `match` on the option: `none` (empty edge list, i.e. trivial walk) returns `False`; `some a` delegates to `G.into v a`. This `getLast?`-based access pattern lets the predicate's definitional unfolding be one `List.getLast?` lookup rather than `Walk.length p` recursion steps.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Walk classifier: is the last edge `a_{n-1}` an edge into `v_n`?
def intoEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  -- Peek at the last edge via List.getLast?; returns `none` on the empty edge list
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  -- Trivial walk has no last edge
  | none => False
  -- Delegate to `G.into v a` from EdgeRelations.lean
  | some a => G.into v a

lean_blocks.code:
def outOfEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.outOf v a
lean_blocks.explanation: Mirror of `intoEnd` for *out of* `v_n`: `p.outOfEnd` iff `p` is non-trivial AND its last edge `a_{n-1}` is an edge out of `v_n` (def_3_3 item iii), i.e. `a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) ∈ E`. Same `p.edges.getLast?` access as `intoEnd`, but delegates to `G.outOf v a` instead of `G.into v a`. As with `outOfStart`, `G.outOf` excludes `L`-edges, so a bidirected last edge gives `intoEnd ∧ ¬outOfEnd` — again the non-partition shape: the four classifiers are pairwise mutually exclusive in their natural pairings but never jointly exhaustive.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Walk classifier: is the last edge `a_{n-1}` an edge out of `v_n`?
def outOfEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  -- Same `getLast?` access pattern as `intoEnd`
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  -- Empty edge list: trivial walk, vacuously False
  | none => False
  -- Delegate to `G.outOf v a`; excludes L-edges so non-symmetric with `intoEnd`
  | some a => G.outOf v a

lean_blocks.code:
def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
lean_blocks.explanation: Walk-class predicate: `p.IsDirectedWalk` iff every edge of `p` is a *forward* `E`-edge (LN item ii). The recursion is structural: the trivial walk returns `True` (vacuously a directed walk, matching the LN rewrite "the trivial walk is admitted as a directed walk"); a `cons v a _ p` cell requires `a = (u, v)` (forward writing — the direction pin distinguishing this from `WalkStep`'s backward alternative), `a ∈ G.E` (directed not bidirected), and recursively `p.IsDirectedWalk`. Encoded as a `Prop`-predicate on `Walk` rather than a separate inductive type, so a `(p, hp)` pair carries exactly the LN's data and "every directed walk is a walk" is the identity function — no forgetful coercion.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Predicate: every edge is a forward `E`-edge (LN item ii)
def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously directed, per LN rewrite
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- Each step: forward writing `(u, v)`, in `G.E`, and tail is directed too
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsBidirectedWalk
lean_blocks.explanation: Walk-class predicate: `p.IsBidirectedWalk` iff every edge is a *forward* `L`-edge (LN item iii). Structurally identical to `IsDirectedWalk` with `G.L` substituted for `G.E`: vacuously `True` on the trivial walk, and each `cons` step requires the forward writing `a = (u, v)`, membership `a ∈ G.L`, and recursively bidirected tail. Pinning the forward writing (rather than admitting `(u, v) ∨ (v, u)`) follows the LN's primary form; the backward writing is automatically equivalent via `CDMG.hL_symm` (symmetry of `L`), so the more general two-way form is a one-line corollary rather than a primary disjunct.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Predicate: every edge is a forward `L`-edge (LN item iii)
def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- Trivial walk: vacuously bidirected
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- Each step: forward writing, in `G.L`; backward writing equivalent via hL_symm
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsBidirectedWalk

lean_blocks.code:
def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsColliderRest
lean_blocks.explanation: Walk-class predicate for collider walks (LN item iv), case-distinguished on length `n` via nested pattern matching: (i) `nil` (`n = 0`): vacuously `True` — the trivial walk is a collider walk; (ii) `cons v a _ (.nil _ _)` (`n = 1`): the lone edge must be *bidirected*, `a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L`. This is the strict reading per addition `[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note]`, overriding the LN's inline "$v \sus w \in G$" remark which would admit any adjacency; (iii) `cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _))` (`n ≥ 2`): the first edge places an arrowhead at `v_1` (`a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)`), and the rest of the walk satisfies the helper `IsColliderRest` (interior edges bidirected, last edge with arrowhead at `v_{n-1}`).
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Predicate: collider walk per LN item iv (with strict n=1 reading from addition)
def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  -- n=0: trivial walk is vacuously a collider walk
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  -- n=1: lone edge must be bidirected (overrides LN inline note)
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  -- n≥2: first edge has arrowhead at v_1, tail handled by `IsColliderRest`
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsColliderRest

lean_blocks.code: def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup
lean_blocks.explanation: One-liner predicate: `p.IsPath` iff the vertex list `p.vertices` is duplicate-free, encoded via mathlib's `List.Nodup`. The LN's "no node occurs more than once" (item v) is exactly this. The trivial walk's singleton vertex list `[v_0]` is vacuously `Nodup` (via `List.nodup_singleton`), so the trivial walk is a path — matching the LN rewrite's "vacuously a path" reading. Defining `IsPath` over `List.Nodup` (rather than as a fresh structural recursion on `Walk`) inherits decidability, monotonicity, and every other mathlib `Nodup` lemma for free. Index-based pairwise-distinctness (`∀ i < j, vᵢ ≠ vⱼ`) is the equivalent classical form but is non-idiomatic in mathlib.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- A path is a walk whose vertex list has no repeats (mathlib `List.Nodup`)
def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i
lean_blocks.explanation: Main bifurcation predicate (LN item vi), built as a four-way conjunction: (i) `u ≠ v` — distinct end-nodes (LN clause (a) first half); (ii) `u ∉ p.vertices.tail` — `u` does not reappear after position 0 (LN's `v_0 ∉ {v_1, …, v_n}`); (iii) `v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast` — `v` does not appear before the last position (LN's `v_n ∉ {v_0, …, v_{n-1}}`); (iv) `∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i` — there exists a split index packaging LN clauses (b)–(d) plus the operator addition (e). Note this is *weaker* than `IsPath`'s `Nodup`: interior vertices may repeat. `List.tail` and `List.dropLast` are mathlib convenience functions for "drop first/last element".
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- A bifurcation between `u` and `v`: distinct endpoints + a valid split
def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  -- LN clause (a): endpoints differ
  u ≠ v ∧
  -- u appears exactly once (only at position 0); `tail` drops the head
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  -- v appears exactly once (only at last position); `dropLast` drops the tail
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  -- LN clauses (b)–(d) plus addition (e), packaged via the existential split
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i

lean_blocks.code:
def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (x : Node) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x
lean_blocks.explanation: Predicate stating that `x` is *a source* of the bifurcation `p`. Inlines the same three end-node constraints as `IsBifurcation` (`u ≠ v`, `u ∉ vertices.tail`, `v ∉ vertices.dropLast`) and replaces the existential by `∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x`: there is a split index `i` whose hinge is *directed* (per the directed-hinge helper) and at which the post-hinge vertex `v_{i+1}` (LN's `v_k`) is exactly `x`. The `vertices[i + 1]?` notation is Lean 4's `Option`-valued indexed lookup, avoiding in-bounds proof plumbing. Combined with the operator addition, `IsBifurcationSource` automatically forces the source `x` to be an interior vertex (`1 ≤ k ≤ n - 1`), distinct from both end-nodes. A predicate (not an `Option Node` accessor) matches the LN's "we say that the bifurcation has source" reading.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Predicate: `x` is a source of the bifurcation `p` (LN item vi final sentence)
def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (x : Node) : Prop :=
  -- Same endpoint-uniqueness conjuncts as `IsBifurcation`
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  -- Directed-hinge split exists, and the post-hinge vertex `v_{i+1}` equals `x`
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x

addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_right_chain_trivial_is_just_directed_walk] As an additional constraint on the bifurcation form, both endnodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ must have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. In particular, this excludes the degenerate case $k = n$ with directed hinge $v_{n-1} \hut v_n$, in which the walk reduces to a directed walk $v_0 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow v_n$ and $v_n$ has no arrowhead pointing towards it.

[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note] For $n=1$, a collider walk from $v$ to $w$ requires a bidirected edge $v \huh w \in E$ (arrowheads at both endpoints), as dictated by the form $v_0 \suh v_1$ combined with $v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ collapsing onto the single edge. The inline note "$v \sus w \in G$" is to be read in this stricter sense and does *not* admit purely directed edges $v \tuh w$ or $v \hut w$ as collider walks of length 1.
design_choices:
**The walk is encoded as an `inductive Type` indexed by both endpoints, mirroring mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk`.** A flat record bundling a `List (Node × Node)` of edges with a separate coherence proof was rejected because downstream chapters pattern-match on walks at every recursion step; a list-plus-witness pair would force each consumer to destructure the coherence proof one edge at a time. An un-indexed `Walk G` with endpoints in fields would force $\ne$-equalities through every "walks from $u$ to $v$" quantifier. The middle vertex on `cons` is made explicit (departing from mathlib here) so downstream pattern matches reach it without `(v := …)` named-pattern syntax.

**The five derived walk-flavours are `Prop`-predicates on the single `Walk` type, not separate inductives.** Treating each as its own `inductive DirectedWalk` would duplicate the structural recursion and force a forgetful map plus lift-up lemmas in every downstream row; a `Subtype` was dispreferred since it changes walk equality, and a bundled `Bifurcation` `structure` was rejected on similar grounds — the source phrases these as yes/no classifications, and a `(p, hp)` pair carries exactly that information.

**The single-edge constraint is factored into a named helper `WalkStep` rather than inlined in `cons`.** Inlining the two-disjunct `Or` directly into the constructor would double the case count of every downstream recursion that destructures a `cons`-cell. A fourth backward-`L` disjunct is omitted because `hL_symm` collapses it into the forward `L`-case.

**The four end-node classifiers are four independent predicates, not a partition-style enum or a `Bool × Bool` flag.** They are deliberately not jointly exhaustive: the trivial walk satisfies none of them, and a bidirected first edge yields `intoStart ∧ ¬outOfStart`. An enum would need a fifth `none` constructor; a bit-pair would compress two distinct symbols ($\hus$ vs $\tuh$) into bit positions and lose the macro-grep correspondence. Each classifier reuses `G.into` / `G.outOf` from `EdgeRelations.lean` instead of re-spelling the disjunctions.

**Two auxiliary predicates `IsColliderRest` and `IsBifurcationWithSplit` encode the positional case-splits.** An inline single recursion would need boolean flags or length comparisons per `cons`-cell to distinguish first / interior / last edge. Quantifying over an integer split index $k$ and indexing into `p.edges` was also rejected because it would force a $k \le \text{length}\,p$ bound everywhere; the helper's $\mathbb{N}$-counter is decremented by structural recursion and the `nil` branch returns `False`, eliminating bound plumbing.

**A separate `IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit` helper, rather than a `Bool` flag on the general bifurcation predicate.** The two predicates differ on the $n = 1$ case — the general form admits the bidirected hinge, the directed-hinge variant excludes it per the operator addition. A shared predicate with a flag would conflate these. `IsBifurcationSource` is then a `Prop`-predicate on a candidate vertex rather than an `Option Node` accessor, avoiding sentinel values; the source position is read via `p.vertices[i + 1]?` rather than `List.get` with a threaded in-bounds proof.

**The vertex sequence is a `List Node`, not a `Finset` nor a length-indexed `Fin (n+1) → Node`.** A `Finset` is wrong on both counts (the tuple is ordered and may repeat); the indexed function would force `Fin`-arithmetic plumbing at every use site, whereas `List` unlocks mathlib's `Nodup`, `head?`, `getLast?`, `tail`, and `dropLast` API directly — and makes `IsPath` a one-liner over `List.Nodup`.

**Trivial walks satisfy the walk-class predicates vacuously (`True`), but the four end-node classifiers vacuously `False`.** The asymmetry is intentional: directed / bidirected / collider / path explicitly admit the trivial walk, but the classifiers are existentially loaded on $a_0$ or $a_{n-1}$ existing, so `False` is the faithful encoding.

**The $n = 1$ collider case is the *strict* bidirected reading, overriding the source's inline remark.** The relaxed "any adjacency" reading was rejected per the operator addition: the symbolic pattern collapses for $n = 1$ to require arrowheads at both endpoints, forcing the lone edge into `L`. Purely directed edges in either direction are excluded.
